title: NOC 94151 – Camera, platemaking and other prepress occupations | GTR Immigration
description: In 2026, Canada’s prepress sector is at the forefront of the "Digital Workflow" transition. Camera, Platemaking, and Prepress Workers (NOC 94151) are the technical experts who bridge the gap between graphic design and physical production. While traditional "camera" work has largely been replaced by high-speed digital imaging, the demand for Computer-to-Plate (CTP) specialists and digital file-preparation technicians remains high in commercial publishing, packaging, and large-scale newspaper printing. While classified as TEER 4, this role requires a sophisticated blend of traditional printing knowledge and modern software proficiency.

classification.teer: FeatureDetailsBroad Occupational Category9 – Occupations in manufacturing and utilitiesTEER Category4 (Usually requiring secondary school and on-the-job training)Major Group94 – Machine operators, assemblers and inspectorsSub-Major Group941 – Processing and manufacturing machine operators

duties: Digital File Preparation: Operating computer-controlled studio systems to perform color separation, retouching, and layout editing for final printing.
Platemaking: Preparing and initiating the development of printing plates for offset and flexographic processes using CTP devices.
Scanning & Separation: Operating computerized scanning machines or digital cameras to make color separations and corrections from original copy.
Etching & Engraving: Using hand tools or laser processes to engrave text or patterns onto printing cylinders or plates.
2026 Update: Managing "Digital Proofing" workflows where AI-assisted software automatically checks for ink-coverage and registration errors before plates are made.
employment_requirements: Education: Completion of secondary school is mandatory.
Preferred Training: Completion of a College Program in Graphic Arts Technology or specialized industry courses is a significant asset in 2026.
Experience: A combination of on-the-job training and experience in related printing occupations is usually required.
Technical Skills: Proficiency in prepress workflow software (e.g., Kodak Prinergy, ESKO) and advanced color management systems.
job_outlook: The Canadian Occupational Projection System (COPS) expects a balanced market for those who pivot to digital and packaging technologies. While "Film Stripping" is becoming obsolete, the rise of 3D printing prepress and Specialized Packaging Engraving is creating a new, stable niche for technicians who can manage complex digital files.
labour_market_demand: The 2026 outlook for prepress occupations is characterized by a "Limited" overall growth but high demand for technical specialists in high-volume manufacturing:
Ontario (Toronto/GTA): Limited prospects. The region is seeing a decline in traditional newspaper prepress but a steady need for packaging and label-printing technicians.
Quebec (Montreal): Stable demand. Driven by the province's robust commercial publishing and specialized graphics industries.
British Columbia: Moderate prospects. Demand is concentrated in specialized digital prepress for the film industry and sustainable packaging sectors.
National Trend: Employment is increasingly shifting from traditional print shops to in-house marketing departments and specialized "Digital Asset Management" firms.
Why Work in Canada as a Prepress Technician?
High-Tech Production: Work with 2026-grade technologies, including automated CTP lines, high-resolution laser engravers, and AI-driven pre-flighting software.
Specialization in Packaging: Transition your skills into the high-growth sectors of sustainable packaging and corrugated box manufacturing.
Path to PR: Frequently eligible for Provincial Nominee Program (PNP) semi-skilled streams and the 2026 TR to PR transition programs.
Stable Full-Time Work: Over 94% of workers in this NOC are employed full-time, significantly higher than the national average for other manufacturing roles.
pathways: TR to PR Pathway (April 2026): A dedicated 2026 initiative for 33,000 temporary residents. Prepress technicians with 12 months of Canadian experience are high-priority as "Essential Manufacturing" workers.
Ontario Employer Job Offer (In-Demand Skills): Frequently includes specialized manufacturing roles for workers with a valid job offer.
Saskatchewan Hard-to-Fill Skills Pilot: A streamlined pathway for technical machine operators to obtain Permanent Residency with a provincial job offer.
Rural Community Immigration Pilot (RCIP): A high-success pathway in regional printing and packaging hubs where communities are actively recruiting for technical support staff.
